title: Maritime Intelligence: Sanctioned Vessel Networks Supply Syria and Evade Iran Blockade
summary: Maritime analytics revealed expanding sanctioned vessel networks conducting ship-to-ship transfers in the Mediterranean and Persian Gulf, supplying Syria with Russian oil and attempting Iran sanctions evasion.
contents:
## Maritime Risk Analysis: Sanctioned Fleet Operations Intensify

### Syrian Supply Route
Maritime intelligence tracking documented 21 sanctioned vessels delivering Russian crude oil to Syrian ports on a near-weekly basis. The fleet, flagged across Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Comoros, Madagascar, Oman, and Russia, conducts ship-to-ship transfers near Greece, Cyprus, and Egypt to obscure cargo origins.

### AIS Anomalies
SynMax maritime analytics detected irregular tracking behavior among vessels in the supply chain. The Guinea-flagged Aether transmitted intermittently in early January while the Madagascar-flagged Briont broadcast under another vessel's identity from mid-January, both displaying routes consistent with Novorossiysk-to-Syria deliveries.

### Iran Blockade Implications
The US naval blockade of Iranian ports has complicated global tanker operations, with 21 ships turned back to Iran since the blockade's inception. Brent crude volatility — ranging from $110 to $126 per barrel — has incentivized sanctions evasion networks and dark fleet operations.

### Piracy-Maritime Nexus
The diversion of anti-piracy naval patrols to the Hormuz and Red Sea regions has created compounding maritime risk, with piracy surging off Somalia while sanctioned vessel networks exploit reduced surveillance coverage in the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean.

### Sources
Reuters exclusive investigation, SynMax analysis, MarineTraffic data, LSEG ship tracking.
